{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
1. Python Best Practices
   1.1 Code Style & Formatting
   1.2 Project Structure
   1.3 Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
   1.4 Type Hints
   1.5 Error Handling
   1.6 Logging
   1.7 Documentation
   1.8 Performance & Optimization
   1.9 Security Best Practices
2. Python Testing Frameworks
   2.1 Overview & Comparison
   2.2 unittest (Standard Library)
   2.3 pytest
   2.4 nose2
   2.5 doctest
   2.6 Hypothesis (Property-Based Testing)
   2.7 Mocking Libraries
   2.8 Test Coverage Tools
3. Putting It All Together: A Practical Workflow

================================================================================
1. PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 Code Style & Formatting
---------------------------
- Follow PEP 8, the official Python style guide.
  - Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
  - Limit lines to 79 characters (docstrings/comments) and 99 characters (code).
  - Use snake_case for functions and variables, PascalCase for classes,
    UPPER_CASE for constants.
  - Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
  - Use single quotes or double quotes consistently (pick one).

- Use automated formatters and linters:
  - Black: An opinionated, deterministic code formatter.
  - isort: Automatically sorts imports.
  - Ruff: A fast Python linter (replaces Flake8, isort, pyflakes, etc.).
  - mypy: Static type checker.

- Example .pre-commit-config.yaml:
    repos:
      - repo: https://github.com/psf/black
        rev: 24.1.0
        hooks:
          - id: black
      - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
        rev: v0.1.0
        hooks:
          - id: ruff

1.2 Project Structure
---------------------
A well-organized project structure improves maintainability:

    my_project/
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    ├── scripts/
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern project metadata & tool config
    ├── README.md
    ├── LICENSE
    └── .gitignore

- Use the src-layout (src/my_package/) to prevent accidental imports of the
  development version.
- Keep configuration in pyproject.toml (PEP 621).
- Separate tests from source code.

1.3 Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
-------------------------------------------------
- Always use virtual environments to isolate project dependencies.
  - venv (built-in): python -m venv .venv
  - virtualenv: More feature-rich alternative.
  - conda: For data science / scientific computing.

- Dependency management tools:
  - pip + requirements.txt: Simple, widely used.
  - pip-tools (pip-compile): Generates pinned requirements from .in files.
  - Poetry: Modern dependency manager with lock files and packaging.
  - PDM: PEP 621-compliant, fast resolver.
  - uv: Extremely fast, written in Rust (by Astral).

- Best practice: Pin dependencies with exact versions for reproducibility.
  - Use requirements.txt for applications, pyproject.toml for libraries.

1.4 Type Hints
--------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve code clarity and catch bugs early.
- Check types statically with mypy or pyright.

    from typing import Optional, Union

    def greet(name: str, age: Optional[int] = None) -> str:
        if age:
            return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
        return f"Hello {name}."

- Use modern syntax (Python 3.10+):
    def process(items: list[str]) -> dict[str

================================================================================
1. PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 Code Style & Formatting
---------------------------
- Follow PEP 8, the official Python style guide.
  - 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
  - Limit lines to 79 chars (docstrings/comments) and 99 chars (code).
  - snake_case for functions/variables, PascalCase for classes,
    UPPER_CASE for constants.
  - Two blank lines around top-level function/class definitions.

- Use automated formatters and linters:
  - Black: Opinionated, deterministic code formatter.
  - isort: Automatically sorts imports.
  - Ruff: Fast Python linter (replaces Flake8, isort, pyflakes).
  - mypy: Static type checker.

- Use pre-commit hooks to enforce style before commits:
    repos:
      - repo: https://github.com/psf/black
        rev: 24.1.0
        hooks:
          - id: black
      - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit
        rev: v0.1.0
        hooks:
          - id: ruff

1.2 Project Structure
---------------------
A well-organized project structure:

    my_project/
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    ├── scripts/
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Project metadata & tool config (PEP 621)
    ├── README.md
    ├── LICENSE
    └── .gitignore

- Use the src-layout (src/my_package/) to prevent accidental imports
  of the development version.
- Keep configuration in pyproject.toml.
- Separate tests from source code.

1.3 Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
-------------------------------------------------
- Always use virtual environments to isolate project dependencies.
  - venv (built-in): python -m venv .venv
  - virtualenv: More feature-rich alternative.
  - conda: For data science / scientific computing.

- Dependency management tools:
  - pip + requirements.txt: Simple, widely used.
  - pip-tools (pip-compile): Generates pinned requirements from .in files.
  - Poetry: Modern dependency manager with lock files and packaging.
  - PDM: PEP 621-compliant, fast resolver.
  - uv: Extremely fast, written in Rust (by Astral).

- Best practice: Pin dependencies with exact versions for reproducibility.
  Use requirements.txt for applications, pyproject.toml for libraries.

1.4 Type Hints
--------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve clarity and catch bugs early.
- Check types statically with mypy or pyright.

    from typing import Optional

    def greet(name: str, age: Optional[int] = None) -> str:
        if age:
            return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
        return f"Hello {name}."

- Modern syntax (Python 3.10+):
    def process(items: list[str]) -> dict[str, int]:
        return {item: len(item) for item in items}

- Use Protocol classes for structural subtyping.
- Use Literal types for constrained values.
- Use TypedDict for typed dictionaries.
- Use dataclasses for simple data containers.
